search_results.title: iaincollins/structured-data-testing-tool - GitHub
search_results.snippet: Inspect and test web pages for Structured Data. Includes both a Command Line Interface for easy ad-hoc testing of URLs and library with extendable API.

message.content:
Below is an **exhaustive list of Google’s general (non–type-specific) structured‑data guidelines and policies that can be turned into concrete checks**, together with:

- the **check** (how you’d implement/test it),
- **what it verifies**, and  
- the **exact Google Search Central URL**.

All items below come from Google’s **General structured data guidelines** page unless noted otherwise.

---

### 1. Use only Google‑supported formats (JSON‑LD, Microdata, RDFa)

**Check**  
Verify that all structured data on the page is in one of these formats:  
- `application/ld+json` JSON‑LD script blocks, or  
- Microdata (itemscope/itemtype/itemprop), or  
- RDFa attributes.  
Fail if any other structured‑data format is used for Google‑eligible content.

**What it verifies**  
Confirms that structured data is in one of the **three formats supported for rich result eligibility**: **JSON‑LD (recommended), Microdata, or RDFa**.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 2. Use JSON‑LD as the preferred format (non‑hard requirement)

**Check**  
Informational/quality check: if Microdata or RDFa is used and JSON‑LD is feasible, flag as an improvement (“Google recommends JSON‑LD”).

**What it verifies**  
Implements Google’s **recommendation** (not a strict requirement) that **JSON‑LD is the preferred format** for structured data.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 3. Structured data must represent the main visible page content (true representation)

**Check**  
For each entity described in structured data, confirm that:  
- the **same entity and key attributes are clearly visible** in the main page content (e.g., product name, price, event details, review ratings),  
- there are no entities in structured data that have **no corresponding visible content**.  
Flag if structured data describes entities or details absent from the page.

**What it verifies**  
Ensures that **structured data is a true representation of the page content** and not disconnected from what users see.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 4. No markup for hidden content (no markup for non‑visible or concealed data)

**Check**  
- Detect structured data describing text, prices, ratings, events, etc. that are **not present in visible DOM** for typical users.  
- Flag if the relevant content is present only in hidden elements (e.g., `display:none`, hidden tabs not accessible, or only in comments/template code).

**What it verifies**  
Enforces that **structured data represents the content that users actually see** and is **not used to mark up hidden content**, which would violate the “true representation of the page content” expectation and anti‑deception rules.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 5. No irrelevant or misleading markup

**Check**  
- Compare entity type and fields to the page’s primary topic.  
- Flag cases such as:
  - `Product` schema on a blog post that is not selling that product.  
  - `Review` or `AggregateRating` for items not reviewed on the page.  
  - Fake or exaggerated ratings (e.g., hard‑coded 5.0 stars without visible review content).

**What it verifies**  
Ensures you **don’t mark up irrelevant or misleading content**, such as fake reviews or entities unrelated to the page focus, and **don’t use structured data to deceive or mislead users**.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 6. Follow Google Search spam policies (general content / spam policies)

**Check**  
High‑level policy check rather than strict automation:  
- Confirm structured data does **not participate in spam behaviors**, including:
  - cloaked or deceptive content in structured data,  
  - manipulative use of ratings, job postings, etc.,  
  - structured data used to promote spammy or policy‑violating content.  
- Optionally cross‑check against Google Search **Spam policies** documents.

**What it verifies**  
Requires structured data to **follow the spam policies for Google web search** as part of rich‑result eligibility.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies` (plus linked Spam policies for web search)[5]

---

### 7. Provide up‑to‑date, time‑relevant information

**Check**  
For time‑sensitive markup (events, job postings, offers, etc.):  
- Check dates and status fields to ensure they are **not clearly expired or out of date** (e.g., event date in the past while still marked as upcoming).  
- Flag obviously stale structured data that is inconsistent with the page state.

**What it verifies**  
Implements the rule to **provide up‑to‑date information** and that **Google won’t show rich results for time‑sensitive content that is no longer relevant**.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 8. Provide original content (no scraped or duplicate-only structured data)

**Check**  
Policy/quality check:  
- Identify if structured data references content that appears to be **purely copied** from other sites without adding value, or uses third‑party reviews/ratings without attribution or permission.  
- Flag if the page itself contains little to no original content while structured data is extensive.

**What it verifies**  
Requires that structured data describes **original content that you or your users have generated**, aligning with Google’s quality expectations.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 9. No impersonation or misrepresentation in structured data

**Check**  
- Validate that `Organization`, `Person`, `sameAs`, `author`, `publisher`, etc. **correctly identify the site or content owner**.  
- Flag if structured data claims affiliation or identity (brand, person, organization) that conflicts with visible branding or ownership on the page.

**What it verifies**  
Ensures you **don’t impersonate any person or organization, or misrepresent your ownership, affiliation, or primary purpose** via structured data.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 10. Respect additional content guidelines of each feature

**Check**  
Meta‑check at configuration level:  
- When a specific rich result type is used (e.g., `JobPosting`, `FAQPage`, `Review`), ensure that **the page and structured data also follow that feature’s specific content/policy guidelines** (e.g., not marking up content that’s not user‑visible, appropriate usage of FAQs, etc.).  
- Implementation: link each type to its corresponding Search Central feature doc and validate high‑level constraints.

**What it verifies**  
Follows Google’s requirement that **content in structured data must also comply with any additional content guidelines or policies documented in the specific feature guide**.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies` (plus type‑specific guides linked from this page)[5]

---

### 11. All required properties present for each rich result type

**Check**  
For each supported rich‑result type detected:  
- Retrieve the **required properties** from Google’s feature documentation.  
- Validate that **all required properties are present and syntactically correct** in the structured data.  
- Flag missing required properties as errors.

**What it verifies**  
Confirms that you **specify all required properties listed in the documentation** for the specific rich result type; items missing required properties **are not eligible** for rich results.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 12. Provide recommended properties where possible (quality, not eligibility)

**Check**  
- For each detected type, evaluate **recommended properties** per Google’s docs.  
- Flag missing recommended properties as **improvements** (not blocking).

**What it verifies**  
Aligns with the guideline that **the more recommended properties you provide, the higher quality the result is to users**.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 13. Use the most specific applicable Schema.org type

**Check**  
- Inspect the `@type` (or `itemtype`) used.  
- Verify that, given the visible content, a **more specific subtype** exists and is appropriate (e.g., `NewsArticle` vs generic `Article`, `Product` vs generic `Thing`).  
- Flag cases where only overly broad types (like `Thing`) are used when a clear specific type fits.

**What it verifies**  
Implements Google’s guidance to **use the most specific applicable type and property names defined by schema.org for your markup**.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 14. Place the structured data on the page that it describes

**Check**  
- Ensure that the entity in structured data (URL, name, content) matches the **page being crawled**, not a different page, except where feature docs explicitly allow remote markup.  
- Flag structured data that describes content solely located on another URL (e.g., a product on a different site) unless allowed by a specific feature’s guidelines.

**What it verifies**  
Follows the **location** guideline: **put the structured data on the page that it describes**, unless documentation specifies otherwise.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 15. Duplicate pages must carry consistent structured data

**Check**  
- For sets of duplicate or near‑duplicate pages (e.g., via canonical), verify that **structured data is consistent** across them.  
- Flag where canonical and non‑canonical variants have different or incomplete structured data for the same content.

**What it verifies**  
Implements the recommendation that if you have duplicate pages, you **place the same structured data on all page duplicates, not just on the canonical page**.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 16. Technical guideline – pages with structured data must be crawlable and accessible

**Check**  
- Confirm the page with structured data is **not blocked to Googlebot** by:
  - `robots.txt`,  
  - `noindex` robots meta tag, or  
  - other access-control methods (e.g., login walls without proper allowances).  
- You can emulate what Google recommends using the URL Inspection and Rich Results Test tools.

**What it verifies**  
Satisfies the **Access** technical guideline: **don’t block your structured data pages to Googlebot using robots.txt, `noindex`, or other access controls**.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 17. Technical guideline – structured data must be on the HTML Google actually fetches

**Check**  
Although not on the general guidelines page, this is a core technical rule in Google’s official Merchant Center structured‑data help doc:  
- Fetch the page as Googlebot (no JS execution) and confirm that **structured data is present in the raw HTML response**.  
- Flag if structured data is inserted **only via client‑side JavaScript after load** and not visible to Google’s HTML fetch.

**What it verifies**  
Implements the rule that **structured data markup must be present in the HTML returned from the web server; it cannot rely solely on client‑side JS to generate it after page load**, in contexts like Merchant Center.[7]

**URL**  
`https://support.google.com/merchants/answer/7331077`[7]

---

### 18. Technical guideline – images in structured data must be relevant and crawlable/indexable

**Check**  
For properties like `image`:  
- Check that the **image is relevant to the page’s topic/entity** (e.g., product photo for a Product page, article image for a NewsArticle).  
- Verify that the **image URL is not blocked by robots.txt**, and does not return 4xx/5xx.  
- Confirm the image URL is **indexable** (not disallowed, not `noindex` if HTML, etc.).

**What it verifies**  
Ensures:
- Images are **relevant to the page they’re on**, and  
- **All image URLs specified in structured data are crawlable and indexable**, otherwise Google can’t show them in search results.[5]

**URL**  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

### 19. Use Google’s tools to test technical compliance (Rich Results Test, URL Inspection)

**Check**  
Meta‑check/tooling guidance:  
- Incorporate automated calls or links to:
  - **Rich Results Test** for rich result eligibility, and  
  - **URL Inspection tool** to check crawling/indexability.  
- Report surfaced issues (errors, ineligible markup) from these tools.

**What it verifies**  
Aligns with Google’s recommendation that you **test compliance with technical guidelines using the Rich Results Test and the URL Inspection tool**, which catch most technical errors.[5][3][8]

**URLs**  
- General guidelines (tool recommendation): `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]  
- Rich Results Test description: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data`[3]  
- Rich Results Test tool: `https://search.google.com/test/rich-results`[8]

---

### 20. Structured data must not contradict visible values (e.g., prices, dates)

**Check**  
- Compare key structured‑data values (price, availability, dates, rating score, etc.) to **corresponding visible text** on the page.  
- Flag discrepancies (e.g., structured data price different from visible price, availability mismatches, different event date).

**What it verifies**  
Implements the rule (explicitly documented for products, but conceptually general) that **structured data must match the values that are shown to the user**, and providing incorrect data is a violation of Google’s web developer guidelines.[7] It operationalizes the “true representation” and “no misleading content” principles.[5]

**URL**  
`https://support.google.com/merchants/answer/7331077`[7] and  
`https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/sd-policies`[5]

---

These 20 items cover all **general, non–type-specific** structured‑data guidelines and quality/spam constraints from Google that can be expressed as checks, including:

- representation of visible content,  
- anti‑spam / anti‑misleading rules,  
- format and placement rules (JSON‑LD/Microdata/RDFa; on the describing page; in server‑returned HTML),  
- use of correct and complete types/properties,  
- content quality/timeliness/originality, and  
- technical accessibility/crawlability requirements.
